# JSONB null handling in PostgreSQL and Materialize (2026-02-12 19:55Z)

_**User**_

In postgres this query returns null, apparently because any jsonb operation over null should return null.

SELECT NULL::jsonb ? 'key';

But in materialize it returns false, because null rightly does not contain the key.

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is describing a difference in behavior between PostgreSQL and Materialize for the JSONB `?` (contains key) operator when the left operand is NULL.

- **PostgreSQL**: `SELECT NULL::jsonb ? 'key';` returns NULL (because any JSONB operation on NULL should return NULL)
- **Materialize**: Returns FALSE (because NULL doesn't contain the key)

The user seems to be reporting this as something that might need to be fixed - i.e., Materialize should match PostgreSQL's behavior and return NULL when the left operand is NULL.

Let me search for where the JSONB `?` operator is implemented in Materialize to understand the current behavior and how to fix it.
</details></think>

---

Searching for where the JSONB `?` (contains-key) operator is implemented so we can align its NULL handling with PostgreSQL.

        "contents": "fn list_contains_list&lt;'a&gt;(a: ExcludeNull&lt;DatumList&lt;'a&gt;&gt;, b: ExcludeNull&lt;DatumList&lt;'a&gt;&gt;) -&gt; bool {\n    // NULL is never equal to NULL. If NULL is an element of b, b cannot be contained in a, even if a contains NULL.\n    if b.iter().contains(&amp;Datum::Null) {\n        false\n    } else {\n        b.iter()\n            .all(|item_b| a.iter().any(|item_a| item_a == item_b))\n    }\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \"&lt;@\")]\nfn list_contains_list_rev&lt;'a&gt;(\n    a: ExcludeNull&lt;DatumList&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    b: ExcludeNull&lt;DatumList&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n) -&gt; bool {\n    list_contains_list(b, a)\n}\n\n// TODO(jamii) nested loops are possibly not the fastest way to do this\n#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \"@&gt;\")]\nfn jsonb_contains_jsonb&lt;'a&gt;(a: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;, b: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; bool {\n    // https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/datatype-json.html#JSON-CONTAINMENT\n    fn contains(a: Datum, b: Datum, at_top_level: bool) -&gt; bool {\n        match (a, b) {\n            (Datum::JsonNull, Datum::JsonNull) =&gt; true,\n            (Datum::False, Datum::False) =&gt; true,\n            (Datum::True, Datum::True) =&gt; true,\n            (Datum::Numeric(a), Datum::Numeric(b)) =&gt; a == b,\n            (Datum::String(a), Datum::String(b)) =&gt; a == b,\n            (Datum::List(a), Datum::List(b)) =&gt; b\n                .iter()\n                .all(|b_elem| a.iter().any(|a_elem| contains(a_elem, b_elem, false))),\n            (Datum::Map(a), Datum::Map(b)) =&gt; b.iter().all(|(b_key, b_val)| {\n                a.iter()\n                    .any(|(a_key, a_val)| (a_key == b_key) &amp;&amp; contains(a_val, b_val, false))\n            }),\n\n            // fun special case\n            (Datum::List(a), b) =&gt; {\n                at_top_level &amp;&amp; a.iter().any(|a_elem| contains(a_elem, b, false))\n            }\n\n            _ =&gt; false,\n        }\n    }\n    contains(a.into_datum(), b.into_datum(), true)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \"||\")]\nfn jsonb_concat&lt;'a&gt;(\n    a: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;,\n    b: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;,",

    {
      "codeBlock": {
        "relativeWorkspacePath": "./src/expr/src/scalar/snapshots/mz_expr__scalar__func__jsonb_contains_jsonb.snap",

          }
        },
        "contents": "f.write_str(\"@&gt;\")\n    }\n}\nfn jsonb_contains_jsonb&lt;'a&gt;(a: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;, b: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; bool {\n    fn contains(a: Datum, b: Datum, at_top_level: bool) -&gt; bool {\n        match (a, b) {\n            (Datum::JsonNull, Datum::JsonNull) =&gt; true,\n            (Datum::False, Datum::False) =&gt; true,\n            (Datum::True, Datum::True) =&gt; true,\n            (Datum::Numeric(a), Datum::Numeric(b)) =&gt; a == b,\n            (Datum::String(a), Datum::String(b)) =&gt; a == b,\n            (Datum::List(a), Datum::List(b)) =&gt; {\n                b.iter()\n                    .all(|b_elem| a.iter().any(|a_elem| contains(a_elem, b_elem, false)))\n            }\n            (Datum::Map(a), Datum::Map(b)) =&gt; {\n                b.iter()\n                    .all(|(b_key, b_val)| {\n                        a.iter()\n                            .any(|(a_key, a_val)| {\n                                (a_key == b_key) &amp;&amp; contains(a_val, b_val, false)\n                            })\n                    })\n            }\n            (Datum::List(a), b) =&gt; {\n                at_top_level &amp;&amp; a.iter().any(|a_elem| contains(a_elem, b, false))\n            }\n            _ =&gt; false,\n        }\n    }\n    contains(a.into_datum(), b.into_datum(), true)\n}",
        "signatures": {},

        "contents": "---\nsource: src/expr/src/scalar/func.rs\nexpression: \"#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \\\"@&gt;\\\")]\\nfn jsonb_contains_jsonb&lt;'a&gt;(a: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;, b: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; bool {\\n    fn contains(a: Datum, b: Datum, at_top_level: bool) -&gt; bool {\\n        match (a, b) {\\n            (Datum::JsonNull, Datum::JsonNull) =&gt; true,\\n            (Datum::False, Datum::False) =&gt; true,\\n            (Datum::True, Datum::True) =&gt; true,\\n            (Datum::Numeric(a), Datum::Numeric(b)) =&gt; a == b,\\n            (Datum::String(a), Datum::String(b)) =&gt; a == b,\\n            (Datum::List(a), Datum::List(b)) =&gt; {\\n                b.iter()\\n                    .all(|b_elem| a.iter().any(|a_elem| contains(a_elem, b_elem, false)))\\n            }\\n            (Datum::Map(a), Datum::Map(b)) =&gt; {\\n                b.iter()\\n                    .all(|(b_key, b_val)| {\\n                        a.iter()\\n                            .any(|(a_key, a_val)| {\\n                                (a_key == b_key) &amp;&amp; contains(a_val, b_val, false)\\n                            })\\n                    })\\n            }\\n            (Datum::List(a), b) =&gt; {\\n                at_top_level &amp;&amp; a.iter().any(|a_elem| contains(a_elem, b, false))\\n            }\\n            _ =&gt; false,\\n        }\\n    }\\n    contains(a.into_datum(), b.into_datum(), true)\\n}\\n\"\n---\n#[derive(\n    proptest_derive::Arbitrary,\n    Ord,\n    PartialOrd,\n    Clone,\n    Debug,\n    Eq,\n    PartialEq,\n    serde::Serialize,\n    serde::Deserialize,\n    Hash,\n    mz_lowertest::MzReflect\n)]\npub struct JsonbContainsJsonb;\nimpl&lt;'a&gt; crate::func::binary::EagerBinaryFunc&lt;'a&gt; for JsonbContainsJsonb {\n    type Input1 = JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;;\n    type Input2 = JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;;",

Option&lt;&amp;'a str&gt; {\n    let v = jsonb_get_string(a, k)?;\n    jsonb_stringify(v.into_datum(), temp_storage)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(sqlname = \"#&gt;\", is_infix_op = true)]\nfn jsonb_get_path&lt;'a&gt;(mut json: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;, b: Array&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; Option&lt;JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;&gt; {\n    let path = b.elements();\n    for key in path.iter() {\n        let key = match key {\n            Datum::String(s) =&gt; s,\n            Datum::Null =&gt; return None,\n            _ =&gt; unreachable!(\"keys in jsonb_get_path known to be strings\"),\n        };\n        let v = match json.into_datum() {\n            Datum::Map(map) =&gt; map.iter().find(|(k, _)| key == *k).map(|(_k, v)| v),\n            Datum::List(list) =&gt; {\n                let i = strconv::parse_int64(key).ok()?;\n                let i = if i &gt;= 0 {\n                    usize::cast_from(i.unsigned_abs())\n                } else {\n                    // index backwards from the end\n                    let i = usize::cast_from(i.unsigned_abs());\n                    (list.iter().count()).wrapping_sub(i)\n                };\n                list.iter().nth(i)\n            }\n            _ =&gt;         "contents": " else {\n        Ok(like)\n    }\n}\n\nfn plan_subscript_jsonb(\n    ecx: &amp;ExprContext,\n    expr: HirScalarExpr,\n    positions: &amp;[SubscriptPosition&lt;Aug&gt;],\n) -&gt; Result&lt;CoercibleScalarExpr, PlanError&gt; {\n    use CastContext::Implicit;\n    use SqlScalarType::{Int64, String};\n\n    // JSONB doesn't support the slicing syntax, so simply error if you\n    // encounter any explicit slices.\n    let subscripts = extract_scalar_subscript_from_positions(positions, \"jsonb\")?;\n\n    let mut exprs = Vec::with_capacity(subscripts.len());\n    for s in subscripts {\n        let subscript = plan_expr(ecx, s)?;\n        let subscript = if let Ok(subscript) = subscript.clone().cast_to(ecx, Implicit, &amp;String) {\n            subscript\n        } else if let Ok(subscript) = subscript.cast_to(ecx, Implicit, &amp;Int64) {\n            // Integers are converted to a string here and then re-parsed as an\n            // integer by &#96;JsonbGetPath&#96;. Weird, but this is how PostgreSQL says to\n            // do it.\n            typeconv::to_string(ecx, subscript)\n        } else {\n            sql_bail!(\"jsonb subscript type must be coercible to integer or text\");\n        };\n        exprs.push(subscript);\n    }\n\n    // Subscripting works like &#96;expr #&gt; ARRAY[subscript]&#96; rather than\n    // &#96;expr-&gt;subscript&#96; as you might expect.\n    let expr = expr.call_binary(\n        HirScalarExpr::call_variadic(\n            VariadicFunc::ArrayCreate {\n                elem_type: SqlScalarType::String,\n            },\n            exprs,\n        ),\n        expr_func::JsonbGetPath,\n    );\n    Ok(expr.into())\n}\n\nfn plan_exists(ecx: &amp;ExprContext, query: &amp;Query&lt;Aug&gt;) -&gt; Result&lt;CoercibleScalarExpr, PlanError&gt; {\n    if !ecx.allow_subqueries {\n        sql_bail!(\"{} does not allow subqueries\", ecx.name)\n    }\n    let mut qcx = ecx.derived_query_context();\n    let (expr, _scope) = plan_nested_query(&amp;mut qcx, query)?;\n    Ok(expr.exists().into())\n}\n\nfn plan_subquery",

        "contents": "#[sqlfunc(propagates_nulls = true)]\nfn get_bit(bytes: &amp;[u8], index: i32) -&gt; Result&lt;i32, EvalError&gt; {\n    let err = EvalError::IndexOutOfRange {\n        provided: index,\n        valid_end: i32::try_from(bytes.len().saturating_mul(8)).unwrap() - 1,\n    };\n\n    let index = usize::try_from(index).map_err(|_| err.clone())?;\n\n    let byte_index = index / 8;\n    let bit_index = index % 8;\n\n    let i = bytes\n        .get(byte_index)\n        .map(|b| (*b &gt;&gt; bit_index) &amp; 1)\n        .ok_or(err)?;\n    assert!(i == 0 || i == 1);\n    Ok(i32::from(i))\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(propagates_nulls = true)]\nfn get_byte(bytes: &amp;[u8], index: i32) -&gt; Result&lt;i32, EvalError&gt; {\n    let err = EvalError::IndexOutOfRange {\n        provided: index,\n        valid_end: i32::try_from(bytes.len()).unwrap() - 1,\n    };\n    let i: &amp;u8 = bytes\n        .get(usize::try_from(index).map_err(|_| err.clone())?)\n        .ok_or(err)?;\n    Ok(i32::from(*i))\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(sqlname = \"constant_time_compare_bytes\", propagates_nulls = true)]\npub fn constant_time_eq_bytes(a: &amp;[u8], b: &amp;[u8]) -&gt; bool {\n    bool::from(a.ct_eq(b))\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(sqlname = \"constant_time_compare_strings\", propagates_nulls = true)]\npub fn constant_time_eq_string(a: &amp;str, b: &amp;str) -&gt; bool {\n    bool::from(a.as_bytes().ct_eq(b.as_bytes()))\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \"@&gt;\", propagates_nulls = true)]\nfn range_contains_i32&lt;'a&gt;(a: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;, b: i32) -&gt; bool {\n    a.contains_elem(&amp;b)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \"@&gt;\", propagates_nulls = true)]\nfn range_contains_i64&lt;'a&gt;(a: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;, elem: i64) -&gt; bool {\n    a.contains_elem(&amp;elem)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \"@&gt;\", propagates_nulls = true)]\nfn range_contains_numeric&lt;'a&gt;(a: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;, elem: OrderedDecimal&lt;Numeric&gt;) -&gt;         "contents": "# Copyright Materialize, Inc. and contributors. All rights reserved.\n#\n# Use of this software is governed by the Business Source License\n# included in the LICENSE file.\n#\n# As of the Change Date specified in that file, in accordance with\n# the Business Source License, use of this software will be governed\n# by the Apache License, Version 2.0.\n\n# An interpret test case specifies, in order:\n# - The column types.\n# - Literal values for each column. Our spec for that column will be the union of the\n#   specs of the column's values.\n# - The expression. We'll interpret the expression to get an output spec.\n# - Literal values to test the output spec against.\n\n# Is this thing on? Check that basic integer ranges work as expected.\ninterpret\n[int32]\n[\n  [4 6]\n]\n#0\n[3 5 7 (\"test\" string)]\n----\nmay contain: [5]\n\n# A temporal-style filter: compare a value to some function of the column.\n# Expression: (2100 &gt;= 300 + insert_ms)\ninterpret\n[string numeric]\n[\n  [(\"hello\" string) (\"goodbye\" string)]\n  [(2000.0 numeric) (2050.0 numeric)]\n]\n(call_binary gte (2100.0 numeric) (call_binary add_numeric (300.0 numeric) #1))\n[true false null]\n----\nmay contain: [false]\n\n# The same, but the filter matches.\n# Expression: (2900 &gt;= 300 + insert_ms)\ninterpret\n[string numeric]\n[\n  [(\"hello\" string) (\"goodbye\" string)]\n  [(2000.0 numeric) (2050.0 numeric)]\n]\n(call_binary gte (2900.0 numeric) (call_binary add_numeric (300.0 numeric) #1))\n[true false null]\n----\nmay contain: [true]\n\n# JSONB -&gt;\n# Expression: (json_col -&gt; \"created_ms\")\ninterpret\n[jsonb]\n[\n  [(\"{\\\"created_ms\\\": 1000}\" jsonb) (\"{\\\"created_ms\\\": 2000}\" jsonb) (\"{\\\"created_ms\\\": 3000}\" jsonb)]\n]\n(call_binary (jsonb_get_string) #0 (\"created_ms\" string))\n[(0 numeric) (2050 numeric) (4000 numeric) null]\n----\nmay contain: [2050]\n\n# JSONB -&gt;&gt; (string column)\n# Expression: (json_col -&gt;&gt; \"code\")\ninterpret\n[jsonb]\n[\n  [(\"{\\\"code\\\": \\\"00135\\\"}\" jsonb) (\"{\\\"code\\\": \\\"22122\\\"}\" jsonb) (\"{\\\"code\\\": \\\"34153\\\"}\" jsonb)]\n]\n(call_binary (jsonb_get_string_stringify) #0 (\"code\" string))\n[\"00000\" \"20000\" \"2\" \"80000\" null]\n----\nmay contain: [\"20000\" \"2\"]",

        "contents": "#[allow(clippy::as_conversions)]\nfn array_upper&lt;'a&gt;(a: Array&lt;'a&gt;, i: i64) -&gt; Result&lt;Option&lt;i32&gt;, EvalError&gt; {\n    if i &lt; 1 {\n        return Ok(None);\n    }\n    a.dims()\n        .into_iter()\n        .nth(i as usize - 1)\n        .map(|dim| {\n            dim.length\n                .try_into()\n                .map_err(|_| EvalError::Int32OutOfRange(dim.length.to_string().into()))\n        })\n        .transpose()\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    is_infix_op = true,\n    sqlname = \"array_contains\",\n    propagates_nulls = true,\n    introduces_nulls = false\n)]\nfn array_contains&lt;'a&gt;(a: Datum&lt;'a&gt;, array: Array&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; bool {\n    array.elements().iter().any(|e| e == a)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \"@&gt;\")]\nfn array_contains_array&lt;'a&gt;(a: Array&lt;'a&gt;, b: Array&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; bool {\n    let a = a.elements();\n    let b = b.elements();\n\n    // NULL is never equal to NULL. If NULL is an element of b, b cannot be contained in a, even if a contains NULL.\n    if b.iter().contains(&amp;Datum::Null) {\n        false\n    } else {\n        b.iter()\n            .all(|item_b| a.iter().any(|item_a| item_a == item_b))\n    }\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \"&lt;@\")]\nfn array_contains_array_rev&lt;'a&gt;(a: Array&lt;'a&gt;, b: Array&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; bool {\n    array_contains_array(b, a)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    output_type_expr = \"input_type_a.scalar_type.without_modifiers().nullable(true)\",\n    is_infix_op = true,\n    sqlname = \"||\",\n    propagates_nulls = false,\n    introduces_nulls = false\n)]\nfn array_array_concat&lt;'a&gt;(\n    a: Datum&lt;'a&gt;,\n    b: Datum&lt;'a&gt;,\n    temp_storage: &amp;'a RowArena,\n) -&gt;",

        "contents": "    fn test_jsonb() {\n        let arena = RowArena::new();\n\n        let expr = MirScalarExpr::column(0)\n            .call_binary(\n                MirScalarExpr::Literal(\n                    Ok(Row::pack_slice(&amp;[\"ts\".into()])),\n                    SqlScalarType::String.nullable(false),\n                ),\n                JsonbGetString,\n            )\n            .call_unary(CastJsonbToNumeric(None));\n\n        let relation = SqlRelationType::new(vec![SqlScalarType::Jsonb.nullable(true)]);\n        let mut interpreter = ColumnSpecs::new(&amp;relation, &amp;arena);\n        interpreter.push_column(\n            0,\n            ResultSpec::map_spec(\n                [(\n                    \"ts\".into(),\n                    ResultSpec::value_between(\n                        Datum::Numeric(100.into()),\n                        Datum::Numeric(300.into()),\n                    ),\n                )]\n                .into_iter()\n                .collect(),\n            ),\n        );\n\n        let range_out = interpreter.expr(&amp;expr).range;\n        assert!(!range_out.may_contain(Datum::Numeric(0.into())));\n        assert!(range_out.may_contain(Datum::Numeric(200.into())));\n        assert!(!range_out.may_contain(Datum::Numeric(400.into())));\n    }\n\n    #[mz_ore::test]\n    fn test_like() {\n        let arena = RowArena::new();",

        "contents": "---\nsource: src/expr/src/scalar/func.rs\nexpression: \"#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \\\"?\\\", propagates_nulls = true)]\\nfn map_contains_key&lt;'a&gt;(map: DatumMap&lt;'a&gt;, k: &amp;str) -&gt; bool {\\n    map.iter().any(|(k2, _v)| k == k2)\\n}\\n\"\n---\n#[derive(\n    proptest_derive::Arbitrary,\n    Ord,\n    PartialOrd,\n    Clone,\n    Debug,\n    Eq,\n    PartialEq,\n    serde::Serialize,\n    serde::Deserialize,\n    Hash,\n    mz_lowertest::MzReflect\n)]\npub struct MapContainsKey;\nimpl&lt;'a&gt; crate::func::binary::EagerBinaryFunc&lt;'a&gt; for MapContainsKey {\n    type Input1 = DatumMap&lt;'a&gt;;\n    type Input2 = &amp;'a str;\n    type Output = bool;\n    fn call(\n        &amp;self,\n        a: Self::Input1,\n        b: Self::Input2,\n        temp_storage: &amp;'a mz_repr::RowArena,\n    ) -&gt; Self::Output {\n        map_contains_key(a, b)\n    }\n    fn output_type(\n        &amp;self,\n        input_type_a: mz_repr::SqlColumnType,\n        input_type_b: mz_repr::SqlColumnType,\n    ) -&gt; mz_repr::SqlColumnType {\n        use mz_repr::AsColumnType;\n        let output = Self::Output::as_column_type();\n        let propagates_nulls = crate::func::binary::EagerBinaryFunc::propagates_nulls(\n            self,\n        );\n        let nullable = output.nullable;\n        output\n            .nullable(\n                nullable\n                    || (propagates_nulls\n                        &amp;&amp; (input_type_a.nullable || input_type_b.nullable)),\n            )\n    }\n    fn is_infix_op(&amp;self) -&gt; bool {\n        true\n    }\n    fn propagates_nulls(&amp;self) -&gt; bool {\n        true\n    }\n}\nimpl std::fmt::Display for MapContainsKey {\n    fn fmt(&amp;self, f: &amp;mut std::fmt::Formatter) -&gt; std::fmt::Result {\n        f.write_str(\"?\")\n    }\n}\nfn map_contains_key&lt;'a&gt;(map: DatumMap&lt;'a&gt;, k: &amp;str) -&gt; bool {\n    map.iter().any(|(k2, _v)| k == k2)\n}",

        "contents": "fn jsonb_concat&lt;'a&gt;(\n    a: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;,\n    b: JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;,\n    temp_storage: &amp;'a RowArena,\n) -&gt; Option&lt;JsonbRef&lt;'a&gt;&gt; {\n    let res = match (a.into_datum(), b.into_datum()) {\n        (Datum::Map(dict_a), Datum::Map(dict_b)) =&gt; {\n            let mut pairs = dict_b.iter().chain(dict_a.iter()).collect::&lt;Vec&lt;_&gt;&gt;();\n            // stable sort, so if keys collide dedup prefers dict_b\n            pairs.sort_by(|(k1, _v1), (k2, _v2)| k1.cmp(k2));\n            pairs.dedup_by(|(k1, _v1), (k2, _v2)| k1 == k2);\n            temp_storage.make_datum(|packer| packer.push_dict(pairs))\n        }\n        (Datum::List(list_a), Datum::List(list_b)) =&gt; {\n            let elems = list_a.iter().chain(list_b.iter());\n            temp_storage.make_datum(|packer| packer.push_list(elems))\n        }\n        (Datum::List(list_a), b) =&gt; {\n            let elems = list_a.iter().chain(Some(b));\n            temp_storage.make_datum(|packer| packer.push_list(elems))\n        }\n        (a, Datum::List(list_b)) =&gt; {\n            let elems = Some(a).into_iter().chain(list_b.iter());\n            temp_storage.make_datum(|packer| packer.push_list(elems))\n        }\n        _ =&gt; return None,\n    };\n    Some(JsonbRef::from_datum(res))\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    output_type_expr = \"SqlScalarType::Jsonb.nullable(true)\",\n    is_infix_op = true,\n    sqlname = \"-\",\n    propagates_nulls = true,\n    introduces_nulls = true\n)]\nfn jsonb_delete_int64&lt;'a&gt;(a: Datum&lt;'a&gt;, i: i64, temp_storage: &amp;'a RowArena) -&gt; Datum&lt;'a&gt; {\n    match a {\n        Datum::List(list) =&gt;",

        "contents": "macro_rules! range_fn {\n    ($fn:expr, $range_fn:expr, $sqlname:expr) =&gt; {\n        paste::paste! {\n\n            #[sqlfunc(\n                output_type = \"bool\",\n                is_infix_op = true,\n                sqlname = $sqlname,\n                propagates_nulls = true\n            )]\n            fn [&lt; range_ $fn &gt;]&lt;'a&gt;(a: Datum&lt;'a&gt;, b: Datum&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; Datum&lt;'a&gt;\n            {\n                if a.is_null() || b.is_null() { return Datum::Null }\n                let l = a.unwrap_range();\n                let r = b.unwrap_range();\n                Datum::from(Range::&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;::$range_fn(&amp;l, &amp;r))\n            }\n        }\n    };\n}\n\n// RangeContainsRange is either @&gt; or &lt;@ depending on the order of the arguments.\n// It doesn't influence the result, but it does influence the display string.\nrange_fn!(contains_range, contains_range, \"@&gt;\");\nrange_fn!(contains_range_rev, contains_range, \"&lt;@\");\nrange_fn!(overlaps, overlaps, \"&amp;&amp;\");\nrange_fn!(after, after, \"&gt;&gt;\");\nrange_fn!(before, before, \"&lt;&lt;\");\nrange_fn!(overleft, overleft, \"&amp;&lt;\");\nrange_fn!(overright, overright, \"&amp;&gt;\");\nrange_fn!(adjacent, adjacent, \"-|-\");\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    output_type_expr = \"input_type_a.scalar_type.without_modifiers().nullable(true)\",\n    is_infix_op = true,\n    sqlname = \"+\",\n    propagates_nulls = true,\n    introduces_nulls = false\n)]\nfn range_union&lt;'a&gt;(\n    l: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    r: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    temp_storage: &amp;'a RowArena,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;, EvalError&gt; {\n    l.union(&amp;r)?.into_result(temp_storage)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    output_type_expr = \"input_type_a.scalar_type.without_modifiers().nullable(true)\",\n    is_infix_op = true,\n    sqlname = \"*\",\n    propagates_nulls = true,\n    introduces_nulls = false\n)]\nfn range_intersection&lt;'a&gt;(\n    l: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    r: Range&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;&gt;,\n    temp_storage: &amp;'a RowArena,\n) -&gt; Result&lt;Datum&lt;'a&gt;, EvalError&gt; {\n    l.intersection(&amp;r).into_result(temp_storage)\n}\n\n#[sqlfunc(\n    output_type_expr =",

        "contents": "---\nsource: src/expr/src/scalar/func.rs\nexpression: \"#[sqlfunc(is_infix_op = true, sqlname = \\\"?|\\\", propagates_nulls = true)]\\nfn map_contains_any_keys&lt;'a&gt;(map: DatumMap&lt;'a&gt;, keys: Array&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; bool {\\n    keys.elements()\\n        .iter()\\n        .any(|key| !key.is_null() &amp;&amp; map.iter().any(|(k, _v)| k == key.unwrap_str()))\\n}\\n\"\n---\n#[derive(\n    proptest_derive::Arbitrary,\n    Ord,\n    PartialOrd,\n    Clone,\n    Debug,\n    Eq,\n    PartialEq,\n    serde::Serialize,\n    serde::Deserialize,\n    Hash,\n    mz_lowertest::MzReflect\n)]\npub struct MapContainsAnyKeys;\nimpl&lt;'a&gt; crate::func::binary::EagerBinaryFunc&lt;'a&gt; for MapContainsAnyKeys {\n    type Input1 = DatumMap&lt;'a&gt;;\n    type Input2 = Array&lt;'a&gt;;\n    type Output = bool;\n    fn call(\n        &amp;self,\n        a: Self::Input1,\n        b: Self::Input2,\n        temp_storage: &amp;'a mz_repr::RowArena,\n    ) -&gt; Self::Output {\n        map_contains_any_keys(a, b)\n    }\n    fn output_type(\n        &amp;self,\n        input_type_a: mz_repr::SqlColumnType,\n        input_type_b: mz_repr::SqlColumnType,\n    ) -&gt; mz_repr::SqlColumnType {\n        use mz_repr::AsColumnType;\n        let output = Self::Output::as_column_type();\n        let propagates_nulls = crate::func::binary::EagerBinaryFunc::propagates_nulls(\n            self,\n        );\n        let nullable = output.nullable;\n        output\n            .nullable(\n                nullable\n                    || (propagates_nulls\n                        &amp;&amp; (input_type_a.nullable || input_type_b.nullable)),\n            )\n    }\n    fn is_infix_op(&amp;self) -&gt; bool {\n        true\n    }\n    fn propagates_nulls(&amp;self) -&gt; bool {\n        true\n    }\n}\nimpl std::fmt::Display for MapContainsAnyKeys {\n    fn fmt(&amp;self, f: &amp;mut std::fmt::Formatter) -&gt; std::fmt::Result {\n        f.write_str(\"?|\")\n    }\n}\nfn map_contains_any_keys&lt;'a&gt;(map: DatumMap&lt;'a&gt;, keys: Array&lt;'a&gt;) -&gt; bool {\n    keys.elements()\n        .iter()\n        .any(|key| !key.is_null() &amp;&amp; map.iter().any(|(k, _v)| k == key.unwrap_str()))\n}",

Using `JsonbRef<'a>` (which rejects NULL in `try_from_result`) will make the evaluator propagate NULL. Implementing the fix.

---

Summary of what was wrong and what was changed:

## Cause

For `NULL::jsonb ? 'key'`:

1. The first argument type was `Datum<'a>`.
2. In `binary.rs`, when the left argument is `Datum::Null`, `Datum::try_from_result(Ok(Datum::Null))` returns `Ok(Datum::Null)` (conversion “succeeds”).
3. The evaluator then calls the function with `Datum::Null`.
4. The implementation matched that on `_ => false`, so the result was `false` instead of NULL.

So the bug was that the type accepted NULL, so the “propagate null” path was never used.

## Fix

1. **`src/expr/src/scalar/func.rs`**  
   The first parameter of `jsonb_contains_string` was changed from `Datum<'a>` to `JsonbRef<'a>`.  
   `JsonbRef::try_from_result` does **not** accept SQL NULL (it returns `Err(res)` for `Datum::Null`), so when the left operand is NULL the evaluator no longer calls the function and instead propagates the NULL (the existing `Err(res) => return res` in `binary.rs` returns `Ok(Datum::Null)` for the overall expression). The body now uses `a.into_datum()` so the logic stays the same for non-null jsonb.

2. **`src/expr/src/scalar/snapshots/mz_expr__scalar__func__jsonb_contains_string.snap`**  
   The snapshot was updated so the generated code uses `JsonbRef<'a>` (and the new body with `a.into_datum()`).

3. **`test/sqllogictest/jsonb.slt`**  
   A test was added that `NULL::jsonb ? 'key'` returns NULL, matching PostgreSQL.

With this, `SELECT NULL::jsonb ? 'key';` returns NULL in Materialize, consistent with PostgreSQL’s rule that jsonb operations on NULL yield NULL.

To confirm, run:

```bash
cargo test -p mz-expr jsonb_contains_string
```

and the jsonb sqllogictests (e.g. the `jsonb.slt` file). If the snapshot diff is only in formatting or path style, you can refresh it with:

```bash
UPDATE_SNAPSHOT=1 cargo test -p mz-expr jsonb_contains_string
```

2. That eval method does `T::Input1::try_from_result(Ok(a))` - when that returns `Err`, it does `return res` which propagates the null/error
3. `JsonbRef::try_from_result` in `src/repr/src/scalar.rs` returns `Err(res)` for `Datum::Null` (and any datum not in the list of valid jsonb variants)

Tracing the exact code path that enforces this:

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /Users/michael.abebe/Code/materialize/src/expr/src/scalar/func/binary.rs</summary>

</details>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2">
<details><summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /Users/michael.abebe/Code/materialize/src/repr/src/scalar.rs</summary>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I have the two key pieces. Let me explain clearly for the user.
</details></think>

---

Two places work together to enforce it:

**1. `JsonbRef` rejects NULL in `try_from_result`**  
`src/repr/src/scalar.rs`:

```2795:2807:src/repr/src/scalar.rs
    fn try_from_result(res: Result<Datum<'a>, E>) -> Result<Self, Result<Datum<'a>, E>> {
        match res {
            Ok(
                d @ (Datum::JsonNull
                | Datum::True
                | Datum::False
                | Datum::Numeric(_)
                | Datum::String(_)
                | Datum::List(_)
                | Datum::Map(_)),
            ) => Ok(JsonbRef::from_datum(d)),
            _ => Err(res),
        }
    }
```

Only those variants are accepted. `Datum::Null` (SQL NULL) is not in the list, so for NULL the match goes to `_ => Err(res)` and the function returns `Err(Ok(Datum::Null))`.

**2. Binary evaluator propagates that `Err` as NULL**  
`src/expr/src/scalar/func/binary.rs` (blanket `impl<T: EagerBinaryFunc> LazyBinaryFunc for T`):

```124:132:src/expr/src/scalar/func/binary.rs
        let a = match T::Input1::try_from_result(Ok(a)) {
            // If we can convert to the input type then we call the function
            Ok(input) => input,
            // If we can't and we got a non-null datum something went wrong in the planner
            Err(Ok(datum)) if !datum.is_null() => {
                return Err(EvalError::Internal("invalid input type".into()));
            }
            // Otherwise we just propagate NULLs and errors
            Err(res) => return res,
        };
```

So when the left argument is NULL:

1. `a` is `Datum::Null`.
2. `T::Input1::try_from_result(Ok(a))` is `JsonbRef::try_from_result(Ok(Datum::Null))` → `Err(Ok(Datum::Null))`.
3. The match hits `Err(res) => return res`, i.e. `return Ok(Datum::Null)`.
4. The binary function is never called, and the expression result is NULL.

So **JsonbRef’s `try_from_result`** is what “rejects” NULL, and the **binary `eval`** is what turns that rejection into “return NULL instead of calling the function.” The comment in `func.rs` is describing this chain.

_**User**_

But shouldn't the NULL be Datum::JsonNull not a SQL Null?

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is making an important distinction:

1. **SQL NULL** – A missing/unknown value. In the repr, this is represented as `Datum::Null`. When you write `NULL::jsonb` in SQL, you're casting SQL NULL to the jsonb type – the *value* is still "unknown/missing", so it's stored as `Datum::Null` (the single SQL null datum), not as a jsonb value.

2. **JSON null** – The JSON literal `null`. In JSONB/repr this is represented as `Datum::JsonNull`. So the JSON value `{"a": null}` has a field whose value is `Datum::JsonNull`. When you parse the string `"null"` as jsonb, you get a value that unpacks to `Datum::JsonNull`.

So:
- `NULL::jsonb` in SQL = "the SQL value is NULL, and we're saying its type would be jsonb" → represented as `Datum::Null` (one variant for all SQL nulls regardless of type).
- `'null'::jsonb` in SQL = the JSON value null → represented as `Datum::JsonNull`.

So the user is asking: when we have `NULL::jsonb`, isn't that actually stored as `Datum::JsonNull`?

No. In SQL and in most type systems, NULL is a separate concept from the value "null" inside a type. So:
- `NULL::jsonb` means "the value is unknown (SQL NULL), and if it were known it would be of type jsonb". That's represented as `Datum::Null`.
- `'null'::jsonb` means "the jsonb value that is the JSON null literal". That's represented as `Datum::JsonNull` when you unpack the row (the jsonb container might still be present in the row format, but the semantic is "this is the JSON value null").

So the user might be confused about the representation. Let me double-check: in Materialize's repr, how is NULL::jsonb stored? It would be stored as a null datum - and the way rows work, a column that is NULL is typically stored with the null bitmap or a sentinel, and when you unpack you get `Datum::Null`. So NULL::jsonb gives you `Datum::Null`, not `Datum::JsonNull`.

The user says "shouldn't the NULL be Datum::JsonNull" - they might think that when you have NULL of type jsonb, it gets represented as JsonNull. But that would conflate "SQL NULL" with "the JSON value null". They are different:
- SQL NULL = absence of value = Datum::Null
- JSON null = the value that is the null literal in JSON = Datum::JsonNull

So the current behavior is correct: NULL::jsonb is SQL null, which is Datum::Null. And JsonbRef::try_from_result correctly rejects Datum::Null (so we don't call the function and instead propagate null). JsonbRef *accepts* Datum::JsonNull because that's a valid jsonb value (the JSON "null" literal).

I should explain this clearly to the user.
</details></think>

---

There are two different things:

1. **SQL NULL** (`NULL::jsonb`) – “no value” / unknown. In the repr this is **`Datum::Null`**. The type `::jsonb` only says “if there were a value, it would be jsonb”; the value itself is still “missing,” so it’s the single SQL null datum.

2. **JSON null** (`'null'::jsonb`) – the JSON literal `null`. In the repr this is **`Datum::JsonNull`**. That’s an actual jsonb value (the one that serializes to `null`).

So:

- `NULL::jsonb` → stored and unpacked as **`Datum::Null`** (SQL null).
- `'null'::jsonb` → stored and unpacked as **`Datum::JsonNull`** (the JSON value null).

`JsonbRef::try_from_result` is doing the right thing:

- It **rejects** `Datum::Null` (SQL NULL) → `_ => Err(res)` so the evaluator never calls the function and propagates SQL NULL.
- It **accepts** `Datum::JsonNull` (JSON null) → that variant is in the `Ok(...)` list, so the function is called. For `'null'::jsonb ? 'key'` you get false (a JSON null value doesn’t contain the key).

So: the NULL in “NULL::jsonb is not accepted” is SQL NULL, and that is represented as `Datum::Null`, not `Datum::JsonNull`. The comment is about that SQL null case.

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

I have the full picture. Let me summarize what the commit does for the user.
</details></think>

---


**Commit f4026873fe** (“[in progress] jsonb changes based on vibes”) does the following:

1. **Makes `NULL::jsonb ? 'key'` return NULL (PostgreSQL-compatible)**  
   Previously the JSONB `?` (contains-key) operator could be called with SQL NULL on the left and returned `false`. The change makes it return NULL when the left operand is NULL.

2. **Implementation**  
   - **`src/expr/src/scalar/func.rs`**: The first parameter of `jsonb_contains_string` is changed from `Datum<'a>` to `JsonbRef<'a>`.  
   - Because `JsonbRef::try_from_result` does not accept SQL NULL (`Datum::Null`), the binary evaluator never calls the function for NULL and instead propagates NULL (as in the existing “propagate nulls” path in `binary.rs`).  
   - The body is updated to use `a.into_datum()` so the logic is unchanged for non-null jsonb. A comment explains that NULL::jsonb is rejected by JsonbRef so the evaluator returns NULL.

3. **Snapshot**  
   - **`mz_expr__scalar__func__jsonb_contains_string.snap`**: New snapshot for the `sqlfunc`-generated code, with `Input1 = JsonbRef<'a>` and the updated function signature/body.

4. **Test**  
   - **`test/sqllogictest/jsonb.slt`**: Adds a test that `SELECT NULL::jsonb ? 'key'` returns NULL, with a comment that in PostgreSQL any jsonb operation on NULL returns NULL.

So the commit aligns Materialize with PostgreSQL for “jsonb op NULL → NULL” by using a non-nullable `JsonbRef` argument so the evaluator propagates NULL instead of calling the function.

---


Here’s a concise summary.

---

## 1. What changed in the snapshot and why

The snapshot is the **generated code** for the `#[sqlfunc]` on `jsonb_contains_string`. It’s produced by the `sqlfunc` macro and checked in so the derive output is stable and testable.

**Concrete changes:**

- **`type Input1`**  
  - Before: `Datum<'a>` (accepted any datum, including SQL NULL).  
  - After: `mz_repr::adt::jsonb::JsonbRef<'a>` (only accepts valid jsonb values; SQL NULL is rejected in `try_from_result`).

- **Function signature and body**  
  - Before: `fn jsonb_contains_string<'a>(a: Datum<'a>, k: &str)` with `match a { ... }`.  
  - After: `fn jsonb_contains_string<'a>(a: mz_repr::adt::jsonb::JsonbRef<'a>, k: &str)` with `match a.into_datum() { ... }`.

**Why:**  
With `Input1 = Datum`, the binary evaluator never saw a “conversion failure” for NULL, so it called the function with `Datum::Null`. The implementation then hit `_ => false` and returned `false` instead of NULL.  
With `Input1 = JsonbRef`, `JsonbRef::try_from_result(Ok(Datum::Null))` returns `Err(res)`, so the evaluator takes the `Err(res) => return res` branch and returns NULL without calling the function. The snapshot just records that the generated code now uses `JsonbRef` and `into_datum()` so the same logic runs only for non-null jsonb.

---

## 2. Other jsonb operators and a more generic approach

**Already correct (first argument is `JsonbRef`, so NULL is propagated by the framework):**

- `@>`, `<@` (contains) — `jsonb_contains_jsonb(a: JsonbRef, b: JsonbRef)`
- `||` (concat) — `jsonb_concat(a: JsonbRef, b: JsonbRef)`
- `->`, `->>` (get by int/string) — `jsonb_get_int64`, `jsonb_get_string`, etc. all take `JsonbRef`
- `#>`, `#>>` (path) — `jsonb_get_path`, `jsonb_get_path_stringify` take `JsonbRef`

So for all of these, `NULL::jsonb <op> ...` already yields NULL because the evaluator never calls the function when the jsonb argument is NULL.

**Use `Datum` but still return NULL on NULL input:**

- `jsonb_delete_int64`, `jsonb_delete_string` — first argument is `Datum<'a>`; they are called with NULL and then return `Datum::Null` in the `_ =>` branch. So the **result** is correct (NULL), but the mechanism is “function handles NULL” instead of “framework propagates NULL.” You could make them take `JsonbRef` for consistency and let the framework propagate NULL; behavior would stay the same.

**The only bug was:**

- `?` (contains key) — it was the only one that both took a type that accepts NULL (`Datum`) and returned a non-null value (`false`) for NULL. The snapshot change fixes that by switching to `JsonbRef` so the evaluator propagates NULL.

---

**Do we need a more generic solution?**

Not for correctness. The pattern “use `JsonbRef` (or any type that rejects NULL in `try_from_result`) for jsonb arguments when you want NULL-in → NULL-out” is already the generic mechanism: the binary evaluator in `binary.rs` is the single place that propagates NULL for all such functions.

Optional improvements:

1. **Consistency:** Change `jsonb_delete_int64` and `jsonb_delete_string` to take `JsonbRef` as the first argument so NULL is handled in the same way as the other jsonb operators (no behavior change, just one place to look for “propagate NULL”).
2. **Documentation:** Add a short comment in `binary.rs` or next to `DatumType` that for “NULL in → NULL out” semantics, the input type should not accept `Datum::Null` in `try_from_result` (e.g. use `JsonbRef` for jsonb), so the evaluator propagates without calling.

A more invasive “generic” approach (e.g. a wrapper or a separate “nullable jsonb” path) isn’t necessary; the current type-based approach in the evaluator is already the generic solution.

The result type varies: ? returns bool, @> and <@ return bool, || returns jsonb, -> returns jsonb, ->> returns text, #> returns jsonb, #>> returns text, - returns jsonb. So I need different query type letters: B for bool, T for text, and for jsonb I need to check - in slt I think we use a type letter. Let me check - query T is text, query B is bool. For jsonb output I'd use something - maybe just a single column. Looking at the file, I see "query T" for JSON output sometimes (e.g. line 735 SELECT '{"a":1}'::JSONB - 'a' -> {}). So the type might be the display type. Let me check - "query T" with result "{}" - so T might be for text? Actually in sqllogictest the type is typically the result type. For jsonb, the result might still be displayed as text. Let me look at how other jsonb-returning queries are typed in this file.

Looking at line 733-735:
query T
SELECT '{"a":1}'::JSONB - 'a'
----
{}

So they use T for queries that return jsonb (displayed as text). So I'll use:
- B for boolean results (? , @>, <@)
- T for jsonb or text results (->, ->>, #>, #>>, ||, -)

I'll add a dedicated section "PostgreSQL: any jsonb operation on NULL returns NULL" and list all the operators.

Summary of what was added:

**New tests in `test/sqllogictest/jsonb.slt`**

Under the existing comment *"PostgreSQL: any jsonb operation on NULL returns NULL"*, these cases were added so that when the **left** argument is `NULL::jsonb`, the result is NULL:

| Operator | Test | Result type |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `?` (contains key) | already had test | bool |
| `@>` (contains) | `NULL::jsonb @> '{"a":1}'` | bool |
| `<@` (contained by) | `NULL::jsonb <@ '{}'` | bool |
| `\|\|` (concat) | `NULL::jsonb \|\| '{}'::jsonb` | jsonb (T) |
| `->` (get) | `NULL::jsonb -> 'key'` | jsonb (T) |
| `->>` (get text) | `NULL::jsonb ->> 'key'` | text (T) |
| `#>` (path) | `NULL::jsonb #> '{a}'` | jsonb (T) |
| `#>>` (path text) | `NULL::jsonb #>> '{a}'` | text (T) |
| `-` (delete by index) | `NULL::jsonb - 1` | jsonb (T) |
| `-` (delete by key) | `NULL::jsonb - 'key'` | jsonb (T) |

So every main jsonb binary operator is now tested with a NULL left-hand side; the letter after `query` is `B` for boolean and `T` for text/jsonb (single column).

If the path syntax for `#>`/`#>>` is wrong for your SQL (e.g. you need `ARRAY['a']` instead of `'{a}'`), the test will fail and you can adjust that literal. Run the jsonb sqllogictest file to confirm.

_**User**_

Explain this:

    // https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/datatype-json.html#JSON-CONTAINMENT
    // NULL::jsonb is not accepted (JsonbRef rejects it), so the evaluator returns NULL.
    match a.into_datum() {
        Datum::List(list) => list.iter().any(|k2| Datum::from(k) == k2),
        Datum::Map(dict) => dict.iter().any(|(k2, _v)| k == k2),
        Datum::String(string) => string == k,
        _ => false,
    }

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user wants me to explain this code block - the jsonb_contains_string function that implements the JSONB `?` (contains key) operator. I'll break it down clearly.
</details></think>

---


This implements the JSONB **`?` (contains key)** operator: it returns whether the JSON value “contains” the given key `k`.

**What each part does**

1. **Comments**
   - First: links to PostgreSQL’s JSON containment docs.
   - Second: why `NULL::jsonb` gives NULL. The function takes `JsonbRef`, so SQL NULL is rejected before the function runs and the evaluator returns NULL. So this function is only called when the left argument is a real jsonb value.

2. **`a.into_datum()`**
   - `a` is a `JsonbRef` (borrowed view of jsonb). `into_datum()` turns it into a `Datum` so we can match on the JSON kind (list, map, string, etc.).

3. **`match` and “contains key” rules**

   From the PostgreSQL semantics:

   - **`Datum::Map(dict)`**  
     Object: “contains key” means the object has a top-level key equal to `k`.  
     `dict.iter().any(|(k2, _v)| k == k2)` checks that.

   - **`Datum::List(list)`**  
     Array: “contains key” means the array has an element equal to the string `k` (array membership, not index).  
     `list.iter().any(|k2| Datum::from(k) == k2)` checks that.

   - **`Datum::String(string)`**  
     String: “contains key” means the string equals `k`.  
     `string == k` checks that.

   - **`_ => false`**  
     Anything else (e.g. number, boolean, json null) is not a container or string, so it does not “contain” a key.  
     Also the fallback if the match is ever extended.

So in plain terms: **“Does this jsonb value ‘contain’ the key `k`?”** — yes for objects with that key, arrays that have `k` as an element, and strings equal to `k`; otherwise no. And because the parameter is `JsonbRef`, **`NULL::jsonb ? k`** never runs this code; the evaluator returns NULL for that case.

_**User**_

But how does taking a Jsonbref and calling into_datum return null?

---

_**Agent (model default, mode Agent)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

The user is asking a good question. If the function takes `JsonbRef` and we call `a.into_datum()`, how can we ever get NULL from that?

The answer is: **we don't**. The function body never returns NULL. When the *left operand* is SQL NULL (`NULL::jsonb`), the value is `Datum::Null`. The evaluator (in binary.rs) tries to convert that to `JsonbRef` via `JsonbRef::try_from_result(Ok(Datum::Null))`. That fails (returns `Err`), so the evaluator **never calls** this function. It immediately does `return res` (returning `Ok(Datum::Null)`), so the *expression* result is NULL without the function ever running.

So `into_datum()` on a `JsonbRef` never returns `Datum::Null` — you only have a `JsonbRef` when the evaluator successfully converted a non-null jsonb datum. The comment is about why the *overall expression* returns NULL when the input is NULL: because the evaluator never calls the function in that case.
</details></think>

---


It doesn’t. **Inside this function, `a.into_datum()` never yields NULL.**

When the left operand is **SQL NULL** (`NULL::jsonb`), the evaluator never calls this function.

Rough flow:

1. Left operand evaluates to `Datum::Null`.
2. The evaluator does `JsonbRef::try_from_result(Ok(Datum::Null))`.
3. `JsonbRef::try_from_result` only accepts real jsonb variants (map, list, string, etc.). It does **not** accept `Datum::Null`, so it returns `Err(res)` with `res = Ok(Datum::Null)`.
4. In `binary.rs`, that `Err` is handled by `Err(res) => return res`, so the evaluator returns `Ok(Datum::Null)` and **never calls** `jsonb_contains_string`.

So:

- **When the function runs:** `a` is a valid `JsonbRef` (non-null jsonb), and `a.into_datum()` is a normal jsonb datum (List, Map, String, etc.). The `match` only sees those; the `_ => false` branch is for other jsonb kinds (number, bool, json null), not SQL NULL.
- **When the result is NULL:** The left argument was SQL NULL, `JsonbRef` rejected it, and the evaluator returned NULL without invoking the function.

The comment is about that second case: “NULL::jsonb is not accepted (JsonbRef rejects it), so the evaluator returns NULL.” It’s describing why the *expression* can be NULL, not something that happens inside this function.

                  "lineNumber": 36,
                  "content": "When planning a one-shot &#96;SELECT&#96;, a &#96;DataflowDescription&#96; is created. A &#96;DataflowDescription&#96; contains both &#96;as_of&#96; and &#96;until&#96; frontiers. The interpretation of these frontiers is that when reading data from a source, a dataflow will fast-forward updates less than the &#96;as_of&#96; to the &#96;as_of&#96; frontier so as to construct a logical snapshot of the input. Then, further updates available in the input will be streamed continuously; however, any updates that are not less than the &#96;until&#96; will be discarded. When the coordinator creates a peek plan, an &#96;as_of&#96; value is provided. Should the planning not hit a fast path, then the &#96;until&#96; value of the &#96;DataflowDescription&#96; will be set to a single-coordinate timestamp conceptually equivalent to &#96;as_of + 1&#96;. This behavior ensures that the input streams produced, e.g., by &#96;persist_source&#96;, will comply with logical, but not physical, monotonicity."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "./doc/developer/design/20230209_invalid_accumulations.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 133,
                  "content": "An alternative approach would be to include checks for the multiset property at the sources, with appropriate measures to surface errors to users when source data violates this constraint. This could be done, e.g., by a design where source data is consolidated to incrementally update a full snapshot of the data at every frontier advancement, followed by an (incremental) test of whether the multiset property has been violated. Only data passing such a test would be fed into a subsequent dataflow computation. Such a design would need to be applied redundantly to introspection sources as well as to persist. <think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

Let me check the insta crate - with insta, you update snapshots by running with INSTA_UPDATE=1. So the flow might be: the sqlfunc macro generates a test that calls insta::assert_snapshot!. When you run the test, insta compares the output to the .snap file. To update, you set INSTA_UPDATE=1 and run the test again. But the jsonb_contains_string doesn't have test=true in the macro - so where does its snapshot come from? Maybe all sqlfunc functions get a snapshot generated by the macro expansion and the snapshot is checked in - and the snapshot is produced by the macro itself (the macro output is what's in the snapshot). So the snapshot might be the output of the macro expansion. In that case, the snapshot would be regenerated when the macro runs - i.e. when you compile. So the "generated code" is the code that the macro expands to - and that's what's in the .snap file. So the snapshot is not "generated" by a test - it's the expected expansion. So when you change the source (func.rs), you run cargo build and the macro expands. But the expanded code is not written to the .snap file by the build - the .snap file is checked in. So there must be a test that runs the macro (or something) and compares to the snapshot. Let me look at how the expr crate tests the scalar func - maybe there's a datadriven test or something that reads the source and expands it.
</details></think>

Actually re-reading the snapshot file - it has "source:" and "expression:" in a YAML frontmatter, then the expanded code. This looks like the format that insta uses when you have multiple snapshots in one file or when you use the "inline" format. Actually the insta snapshot format is typically:
---
source: ...
expression: ...
---
<output>

So the snapshot is the output of the sqlfunc macro expansion. The tests that have test=true generate a test that calls insta::assert_snapshot!. So when you run `cargo test -p mz-expr` with something like INSTA_UPDATE=1, the tests in expr-derive-impl would update *their* snapshots. But the jsonb_contains_string doesn't have test=true - so its snapshot isn't generated by a test in the normal way. Unless... the mz-expr crate has a build script or a test that extracts all #[sqlfunc] from func.rs and runs the expander and writes snapshots? Let me search for that.

                {
                  "lineNumber": 13,
                  "content": "To upgrade your Materialize instances, first choose a new operator version and upgrade the Materialize operator. Then, upgrade your Materialize instances to the same version. The following tutorial upgrades your Materialize deployment running locally on a [&#96;kind&#96;](https://kind.sigs.k8s.io/)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 16,
                  "content": "The tutorial assumes you have installed Materialize on &#96;kind&#96; using the"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 18,
                  "content": "kind](/self-managed-deployments/installation/install-on-local-kind/)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 26,
                  "content": "If you don't have Helm version 3.2.0+ installed, install. For details, see the"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 27,
                  "content": "[Helm documentation](https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install/)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 31,
                  "content": "This tutorial uses &#96;kubectl&#96;. To install, refer to the [&#96;kubectl&#96;"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 46,
                  "content": "Materialize instances are running before the the old instance are removed."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 48,
                  "content": "having both the old and new Materialize instances running."
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "doc/user/content/self-managed-deployments/upgrading/upgrade-on-gcp.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "Kubernetes Engine (GKE). The tutorial assumes you have installed the"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 13,
                  "content": "GCP](/self-managed-deployments/installation/install-on-gcp/)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 34,
                  "content": "- [Terraform](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/install?product_intent=terraform)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 35,
                  "content": "- [Google Cloud CLI](https://cloud.google.com/sdk/docs/install)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 37,
                  "content": "- [Helm 3.2.0+](https://helm.sh/docs/intro/install/)"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 43,
                  "content": "The following procedure performs a rolling upgrade, where both the old and new Materialize instances are running before the old instances are removed. When performing a rolling upgrade, ensure you have enough resources to support having both the old and new Materialize instances running."

                  "lineNumber": 119,
                  "content": "name=\"upgrade-materialize-instance\" %}}"

                  "lineNumber": 11,

                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 20,
                  "content": "When installing the Materialize Helm chart, you can override the [default"
                }
              ]
            },
            {
              "file": "doc/user/content/self-managed-deployments/_index.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 34,
                  "content": "**Orchestration** | [Materialize Operator](#materialize-operator) | Kubernetes operator that manages Materialize instances"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 35,
                  "content": "**Database** | [Materialize Instance](#materialize-instance) | The Materialize database instance itself"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 58,
                  "content": "- To install the Materialize Helm chart and deploy the Materialize Operator and"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 62,
                  "content": "  helm install materialize materialize/materialize-operator"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 72,
                  "content": "- To uninstall the Helm chart (and the Materialize Operator and other"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 76,
                  "content": "  helm uninstall materialize"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 79,
                  "content": "### What gets installed"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 82,
                  "content": "helm install materialize materialize/materialize-operator"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 85,
                  "content": "When you install the the Materialize Helm Chart, it:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 91,
                  "content": "Once installed, the **Materialize Operator** handles the deployment and"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 92,
                  "content": "management of Materialize instances."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 96,
                  "content": "The Materialize Operator (implemented as &#96;orchestratord&#96;) is a Kubernetes operator that automates the deployment and lifecycle management of Materialize instances. It implements the [Kubernetes operator pattern](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/operator/) to extend Kubernetes with domain-specific knowledge about Materialize."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 100,
                  "content": "The operator watches for Materialize custom resources and creates/manages all the Kubernetes resources required to run a Materialize instance, including:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 102,
                  "content": "- **Namespaces**: Isolated Kubernetes namespaces for each instance"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 118,
                  "content": "A Materialize instance is the actual database that you connect to and interact"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 119,
                  "content": "with. Each instance is an isolated Materialize deployment (deployed via a"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 125,
                  "content": "When you create a Materialize instance, the operator deploys three core"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 136,
                  "content": "  Materialize instance. It houses the control plane and contains:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 148,
                  "content": "A Materialize instance manages:"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 159,
                  "content": "To deploy Materialize instances with the operator, create and apply Materialize"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 196,
                  "content": "### Connecting to an instance"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 198,
                  "content": "Once deployed, you interact with a Materialize instance through the Materialize"
                },
                {

The supported comparison operators are &#96;&gt;&#96;, &#96;&gt;=&#96;, &#96;=&#96;, &#96;&lt;=&#96; and &#96;&lt;&#96;. The version number is the same as returned from [&#96;mz_version_num()&#96;](https://materialize.com/docs/sql/functions/#system-information-functions) and has the same format &#96;XXYYYZZ&#96;, where &#96;XX&#96; is the major version, &#96;YYY&#96; is the minor version and &#96;ZZ&#96; is the patch version. So in the example we are only running the &#96;SELECT 1&#96; query if the Materialize instance is of version &#96;v26.2.3&#96; or higher. For lower versions no query is run and no comparison of results is performed subsequently."

            {
              "file": "doc/developer/reference/compute/active-replication.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 3,
                  "content": "The COMPUTE layer employs active replication within clusters, henceforth called instances. The primary goal of active replication is to mask extended recovery times due to dataflow rehydration upon compute crashes. This goal can be achieved by adding enough replicas depending on failure probability and criticality. For example, a customer that can tolerate crash recovery by rehydration might run an instance with a single replica or even for periods of time, have no replica at all in an instance. A customer that wishes to mask compute crashes might run an instance with two or even three replicas."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 5,
                  "content": "Three secondary goals of active replication are: (a) to support the ability to upsize or downsize an instance dynamically, (b) to mask networking issues that either partition away or slow down access to a strict subset of an instance's replicas, and (c) to mask processing issues that increase response latency by again a strict subset of an instance's replicas. For secondary goal (a), upsizing can be achieved, e.g., by adding a larger replica and removing a smaller one, while downsizing, e.g., by adding a smaller replica and removing a larger one. This capability allows customers to adjust their compute costs to their needs over time. For secondary goals (b) and (c), since compute commands are processed deterministically by all replicas, it is only necessary to receive responses from a single replica to make progress. So customers can leverage replication to privilege latency in the presence of certain classes of [gray failures](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/upload [... omitted end of long line]"
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 11,
                  "content": "The active replication mechanism works at a high level as follows. The compute controller maintains a history of compute commands per instance, which it continuously applies to every replica of the instance. Since the replica state resulting from fully applying a command history is deterministic, each replica will arrive at the same state assuming all commands in the compute controller history for that instance are executed in order. A replica is free to maintain a copy of the history or not, depending on its implementation."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 25,
                  "content": "Over time, the compute command history of an instance can grow large. To avoid unbounded growth, the history is reduced, following a length-doubling policy, to an equivalent, hopefully shorter history. The defining property of a reduced history is that its full application by a new replica would bring the replica to the same state as other replicas that have fully applied the unreduced version of the instance's history. More details about history equivalence and reduction are discussed in the [Compute Command Model](compute-command-model.md)."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 29,
                  "content": "Since durability is provided by the ADAPTER and STORAGE layers, the mechanism is intended to tolerate crashes of any number of replicas in an instance. However, masking of recovery delay can only be guaranteed when the compute controller can reach at least one non-faulty replica."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 31,
                  "content": "The compute controller assumes detection of crashes by breakdown of the network communication with a replica, namely when sending commands or receiving responses from the replica. Upon such an assumed detection of a crash, the compute controller adds the replica to the instance's set of presumably failed replicas. The latter makes the replica a target for rehydration, i.e., the process of removing the old replica metadata, re-adding the replica, and sending to the replica the entire reduced history of the instance for replay. Note that when re-adding the replica here, an attempt is made to find an existing incarnation of the replica's service, in which case the [Reconciliation](reconciliation.md) optimization can be triggered; if such an incarnation cannot be found, a new replica service is spawned."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 39,
                  "content": "- [&#96;controller.rs&#96;](/src/compute-client/src/controller.rs): Management of addition / removal of instances as well as core interaction with the ADAPTER layer through the &#96;process&#96; function. Otherwise, all command handling is forwarded to &#96;Instance&#96;s."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 40,
                  "content": "- [&#96;controller/instance.rs&#96;](/src/compute-client/src/controller/instance.rs): Here you find the heart of the replication scheme. The code is very delicate to edit due to the heavy use of asynchrony in &#96;ActiveInstance&#96;; however, a style is used in which nested asynchrony is avoided and each function gets a chance to make a consistent change to the instance's state. Two important data structures are the &#96;Instance&#96;'s &#96;collections&#96; metadata in the form of a map of &#96;CollectionState&#96; and the &#96;Instance&#96;'s compute command &#96;history&#96;. Most API calls will trigger execution of some of the functions &#96;update_write_frontiers&#96;, &#96;remove_write_frontiers&#96;, and &#96;update_read_capabilities&#96; to transition the state of the read and write frontiers kept per collection and replica in response to external triggering of &#96;ActiveInstance&#96; operations. More details about read and write frontiers are described in [Read and Write Frontier Management for Compute Collections](read-write-frontier-management.md)."
                }
              ]
            },
            {

              "file": "doc/developer/platform/reconciliation.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 3,
                  "content": "The controller and compute instances are logically related but can be hosted on different processes, and the state shared between the two needs to be reconciled."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 4,
                  "content": "State includes for example installed dataflows, source subscriptions and sinks, as well as the current progress of the computation."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 5,
                  "content": "On restart, the controller wants to dictate the instances what their state should be."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 6,
                  "content": "The instances want to come up with this state, either by restarting or reconciling their state."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 7,
                  "content": "This document lines out the requirements for reconciling the state compute and storage instances, and active replication."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "Initially, compute instances have no state."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 13,
                  "content": "In the following, we explain what this means for reconciling compute commands on compute instances, storage commands on storage instances, and response reconciliation to enable active replication within the coordinator."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 17,
                  "content": "Commands represent instructions the COMPUTE controller sends to COMPUTE instances."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 18,
                  "content": "Compute command reconciliation (CR) is hosted by each compute instance."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 19,
                  "content": "* The COMPUTE controller may restart independently of a COMPUTE instance."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 20,
                  "content": "* Reconciling commands is the mechanism to match a COMPUTE instance's state to what the COMPUTE controller expects after startup."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 21,
                  "content": "* On startup, the COMPUTE controller provides the current configuration to the COMPUTE instances."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 22,
                  "content": "* COMPUTE instances can match the configuration to their own state and only apply changes."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 37,
                  "content": "* For each installed identifier, CR maintains its upper frontier."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 39,
                  "content": "* CR maintains the set of created instances."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 48,
                  "content": "The command protocol exposes the following verbs to communicate state updates from the controller to a compute instance."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 50,
                  "content": "* &#96;CreateInstance&#96;: If the instance is unknown, remember it and forward the command."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 76,
                  "content": "  This makes it difficult to determine which objects to uninstall once a controller reconnects."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 99,
                  "content": "* We need to validate if STORAGE's timestamp bindings are safe to be stored in memory within the instance until a controller picks them up."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 101,
                  "content": "  For example, when reading a Kafka topic, we create a new topic name each time we instantiate the source."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 108,
                  "content": "Responses represent results and data a COMPUTE instance provides the ADAPTER."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 109,
                  "content": "* Multiple COMPUTE instances can appear as a single unit outwards."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 110,
                  "content": "* Response reconciliation ensures that responses from many COMPUTE instances appear as if it was a single instance."
                }
              ]
            },

            {
              "file": "doc/developer/platform/compute-layer-q-n-a.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 13,
                  "content": "The COMPUTE layer creates a number of sockets to allow for interaction among processes in a Timely instance (COMPUTE replica) in [&#96;src/compute/src/communication.rs&#96;](/src/compute/src/communication.rs). So this module is actually not directly supporting communication with other layers of Materialize, but rather interfacing resources with Timely."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 31,
                  "content": "COMPUTE manages sets of dataflows and arrangements, as well as their interfacing with the other layers of Materialize. Analogously to TD, COMPUTE sources provide data to the COMPUTE layer, while COMPUTE sinks absorb data from the COMPUTE layer. The primary method to enable such data plane transfers from and to the COMPUTE layer is through &#96;persist&#96;, a library that interfaces the COMPUTE and STORAGE layers. In the case of &#96;persist&#96; sinks, the updates are streamed into a persist shard in STORAGE by a tailor-made TD operator, along with relevant frontier advancements. Another option to externalize dataflow results from COMPUTE is through a &#96;SUBSCRIBE&#96; sink. This type of sink leverages a TD sink operator to send updates back to &#96;environmentd&#96; through an instance of &#96;SubscribeProtocol&#96;."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 141,
                  "content": "To deal with this problem, our general strategy to render TopKPlan instances creates a hierarchy of specialized TopK reduce operators. This hierarchy is tuned such that we have a fixed number of logarithmically spaced groupings forming what resembles a tournament structure. In particular, we create 16 stages such that even the largest collection sizes can be handled and at the same time each grouping stage is at first working on groups with a bounded number of records (currently 16) to apply the limit and then increasing the size of the groups."
                }
              ]
            },

                  "lineNumber": 110,

              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 171,
                  "content": "Network policies could be applied at many layers of our stack, from network firewalls or security groups that intercept traffic before it hits application subnets, to k8s or cilium network policies, balancers, or within the database itself. The above solutions choose to implement policies within the database itself. This comes with some disadvantages. For instance, this layer does not have auto-scaling and requires the database to do some work for each denied request. For this reason, it makes sense to shift the policies left. One possible shift is to the balancer layer. In this scenario, balancers would support both HTTP and pgwire load-balancing as well as network policy enforcement. Balancers have auto-scaling and are relatively stateless. A large number of out-of-policy requests to a balancer would likely not impact any ongoing connections. The biggest challenge with implementing network policies in the balancer is that they do not have access to the policies or roles, which are st [... omitted end of long line]"

              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 67,
                  "content": "The primary motivation for enforcing status updates flow through the storage controller is that it allows us to correctly handle instances in which a replica is dropped, in which case any late status updates can simply be ignored and the status of any dataflows managed by the dropped replica can have their status set to &#96;paused&#96;. As mentioned in the [Alternatives](#alternatives) section, relying on graceful shutdown instead would be complex and error prone."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 127,
                  "content": "In order to set a status of &#96;paused&#96; for a source/sink, the storage controller will need to know the dataflow's backing replica at the time the replica is dropped. The mapping between observed sources/sinks currently exists in &#96;mz_storage_client::controller::rehydration::RehydrationTask&#96;, however we can lift it up into &#96;RehydrationStorageClient&#96; in order for the storage controller to access it without duplicating the data. Furthermore, to combat cases in which &#96;environmentd&#96; crashes before it can successfully write a &#96;paused&#96; status, the storage controller should write a status of &#96;paused&#96; for any dataflows that belong to an instance with 0 replicas immediately following coordinator initialization."
                }
              ]
            },

The transformation sets to &#96;false&#96; the &#96;must_consolidate&#96; flag of all monotonic operators with a &#96;must_consolidate&#96; flag previously set to &#96;true&#96; that have an analysis result of &#96;PhysicallyMonotonic(true)&#96; for their input.               "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 118,
                  "content": "Session variables have the problem that a custom value will be lost across environmentd restarts, meaning that it is impossible to install a view where the limit is different from the default, and have the custom limit stick between restarts. (We could conceivably add the value to the catalog along with the query's SQL definition, but this is probably not worth the trouble.) Therefore, a session variable wouldn't be suitable for the 1. (the hard limit) and 3. use cases, because for these one has to raise the limit for a production view in a manner that survives restarts. It might be kind of ok for the 2. use case (debugging): one can temporarily change the limit, and run the query that is being debugged. However, there is still the risk that somebody forgets to change the limit back. Also note that this wouldn't allow for different limits in different WMR blocks inside the same query."

            {
              "file": "doc/developer/design/20230209_invalid_accumulations.md",
              "matches": [
                {
                  "lineNumber": 59,
                  "content": "The approach introduced in the previous section takes advantage of reduction hierarchies with more than one layer, where arrangement reuse for the bottom-most layer is not of concern. However, there are several instances where a single-level reduction is rendered, e.g., for accumulable, basic, or monotonic aggregates. In such cases, it is important to ensure that the output arrangement of the reduction can be reused by downstream operators. However, the closure provided to a Differential Dataflow reduction operator does not produce &#96;Result&#96;, but rather the multiset of aggregated values for a key directly. As such, exposing that an error was detected in the processing logic of the closure is a significant challenge."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 121,
                  "content": "Alternatively, one could introduce a &#96;Datum::Err&#96; variant to signal error. The impact of this change would, however, be non-trivial. Several other parts of the codebase may now have to consider &#96;Datum:Err&#96; as a valid &#96;Datum&#96; variant. Significantly, scalar expression evaluation would potentially need to be changed to match also on &#96;Datum::Err&#96;. Additionally, we would need to revisit the semantics of operators such as joins when they are fed &#96;Datum::Err&#96; instances. For example, one could treat joins in the presence of errors always with outer-join semantics: forward errors without a match on the other join side, or otherwise, compute normally join results. Despite being a viable alternative, the risk and magnitude of the change would be excessively large."
                }
              ]
            },

              "file": "doc/developer/design/20220819_bounded_input_reliance.md",

                  "lineNumber": 9,
                  "content": "The contents of introspection tables are inherently different per timely instance and thus per replica, unlike with other dataflows, it is not expected that different replicas produce the same data."
                },
                {
                  "lineNumber": 37,
                  "content": "At some point in the distant future, we might use this data to automatically detect an overloaded replica and migrate the instance to a bigger instance automatically."
                },

                  "lineNumber": 74,
                  "content": "We have to consider the possibility of a half alive computed that has lost all connections to other workers and environmentd, but still continues writing its introspection sources. At some point, Kubernetes will restart that instance, but this might not happen immediately and we should consider the option of having two computeds - with the same replica id - running at the same time."
                }
